text: A 60-year-old man presents with sudden crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm. He appears diaphoretic. What is the most appropriate first investigation?
rationale: ECG must be obtained within 10 minutes in suspected MI to detect ST elevation and guide reperfusion therapy.

text: Which cardiac biomarker is most specific for myocardial infarction?
rationale: Troponin is the most sensitive and specific marker for myocardial injury. Rises at 3-4h, peaks at 24h, stays elevated 10 days.

text: A patient with acute MI should receive which medication immediately upon arrival?
rationale: Aspirin reduces mortality by inhibiting platelet aggregation. Give 325mg chewed immediately in suspected MI.

text: A patient presents with irregularly irregular pulse, fatigue and palpitations. ECG shows no P waves. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: AF: irregularly irregular rhythm + absent P waves on ECG. Most common sustained arrhythmia. Causes: hypertension, valvular disease, hyperthyroidism.

text: What is the most important complication of atrial fibrillation?
rationale: AF causes stasis in left atrium → thrombus → embolic stroke. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score guides anticoagulation.

text: Which medication prevents stroke in atrial fibrillation?
rationale: Anticoagulation (DOAC preferred over warfarin) prevents systemic embolism in AF. Aspirin alone is insufficient.

text: A systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur radiating to the carotids is most characteristic of which condition?
rationale: Aortic stenosis: harsh systolic murmur at RUSB → carotids. Classic triad: Syncope, Angina, Dyspnea (SAD).

text: A patient presents with hypotension, distended neck veins and muffled heart sounds after a stab wound to the chest. Diagnosis?
rationale: Beck triad = Hypotension + JVD + Muffled heart sounds = Cardiac tamponade. Treatment: pericardiocentesis.

text: Most common cause of heart failure in developed countries?
rationale: CAD (ischemic cardiomyopathy) is #1 cause of heart failure. Hypertension is #2.

text: A 72-year-old with heart failure presents with acute dyspnea and bilateral crackles. Most appropriate first treatment?
rationale: Acute pulmonary edema: IV loop diuretics (furosemide) reduce preload. Also give supplemental O₂ and position upright.

text: Which medication improves survival in chronic systolic heart failure?
rationale: ACE inhibitors (enalapril, ramipril) reduce mortality by preventing cardiac remodeling. Also: beta blockers and spironolactone improve survival.

text: ST elevation in leads II, III and aVF. Which artery is most likely occluded?
rationale: Inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF) = RCA territory. Anterior (V1-V4) = LAD. Lateral (I, aVL, V5-V6) = LCx.

text: A 65-year-old smoker has chronic productive cough for 3 months per year for 2 consecutive years. Diagnosis?
rationale: Chronic bronchitis definition: productive cough ≥3 months/year for ≥2 consecutive years. Part of COPD spectrum.

text: Target oxygen saturation in COPD patients receiving supplemental oxygen?
rationale: COPD target: 88-92%. Higher O₂ suppresses hypoxic drive → CO₂ retention → hypercapnic respiratory failure.

text: Most common cause of bronchiolitis in infants under 2 years?
rationale: RSV = #1 cause of bronchiolitis. Treatment is supportive (hydration, O₂). Palivizumab for high-risk infants.

text: Most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia?
rationale: S. pneumoniae = #1 CAP. Atypical (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella) in young adults. Klebsiella in alcoholics.

text: Best diagnostic test for pulmonary embolism in a hemodynamically stable patient?
rationale: CTPA is gold standard for PE. D-dimer used to RULE OUT PE in low probability. ECG may show S1Q3T3.

text: A patient presents with sudden dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain after a long flight. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Classic PE scenario: immobility (long flight/surgery) + sudden pleuritic chest pain + dyspnea. Risk factors: Virchow triad.

text: Initial anticoagulant treatment of pulmonary embolism?
rationale: Start LMWH (or UFH) immediately. Thrombolysis reserved for massive PE with hemodynamic instability. Warfarin started concurrently but takes days to reach therapeutic level.

text: A patient presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea and vomiting. Serum lipase is 3x upper limit. Diagnosis?
rationale: Acute pancreatitis: epigastric pain radiating to back + elevated lipase (>3x ULN). Atlanta criteria for severity.

text: Most common cause of acute pancreatitis?
rationale: Gallstones #1 (40-70%), Alcohol #2 (30%). Remember: GET SMASHED mnemonic for causes.

text: Most specific laboratory test for pancreatitis?
rationale: Lipase is more specific than amylase. Amylase can be elevated in bowel obstruction, salivary gland disease, renal failure.

text: A patient presents with RUQ pain, fever and jaundice after a fatty meal. Diagnosis?
rationale: Charcot triad: RUQ pain + fever + jaundice = cholangitis. RUQ pain + fever without jaundice = cholecystitis (Murphy sign positive).

text: Most common cause of upper GI bleeding?
rationale: PUD = #1 cause of upper GI bleeding. Varices in cirrhotic patients. All present with hematemesis or melena.

text: Gold standard test for suspected gastric cancer?
rationale: Upper endoscopy + biopsy confirms gastric cancer. H. pylori is major risk factor. Progressive solid dysphagia + weight loss = malignancy until proven otherwise.

text: First imaging test for suspected acute stroke?
rationale: CT head first to exclude hemorrhagic stroke before tPA. MRI more sensitive but slower. Time is brain.

text: Time window for IV tPA thrombolysis in ischemic stroke?
rationale: IV tPA: within 4.5 hours of onset. Mechanical thrombectomy up to 24h for large vessel occlusion. Contraindicated in hemorrhagic stroke.

text: Most common cause of dementia in the elderly?
rationale: Alzheimer = #1 dementia (60-70%). Vascular dementia #2 (stepwise progression). Lewy body = dementia + visual hallucinations + Parkinsonism.

text: Classic presenting sign of Parkinson disease?
rationale: TRAP: Tremor (resting, pill-rolling), Rigidity (cogwheel), Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability. Tx: Levodopa.

text: Most common cause of subarachnoid hemorrhage?
rationale: Spontaneous SAH = ruptured saccular (berry) aneurysm 80%. Thunderclap headache = worst headache of life. CT → LP if CT negative.

text: A newborn develops jaundice on day 3 of life. He is breastfed and otherwise healthy. Most likely cause?
rationale: Physiologic jaundice peaks day 3-5 in term infants (day 2-4 formula-fed). Caused by immature bilirubin conjugation. Treat with phototherapy if approaching exchange threshold.

text: Most common congenital heart defect?
rationale: VSD is #1 congenital heart defect (25-30%). Most small VSDs close spontaneously. Large VSD: left-to-right shunt → pulmonary hypertension → Eisenmenger.

text: Child with 5-day fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, swollen hands/feet and cervical lymphadenopathy. Diagnosis?
rationale: Kawasaki: CRASH criteria = Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue/lip changes, Hand/foot swelling. Dx: fever >5 days + 4/5 criteria. Tx: IVIG.

text: Treatment for Kawasaki disease?
rationale: IVIG (2g/kg single dose) reduces coronary artery aneurysm risk. High-dose aspirin for anti-inflammatory, then low-dose for antiplatelet.

text: First-line treatment for major depressive disorder?
rationale: SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram) = first-line for MDD. Safe, effective, minimal side effects. Start low, titrate. Allow 4-6 weeks for full effect.

text: Mood stabilizer of choice for bipolar disorder?
rationale: Lithium = first-line mood stabilizer. Monitor levels (narrow TI), renal function, thyroid. Toxicity: tremor, ataxia, confusion, seizures.

text: OCD treatment combination?
rationale: OCD: SSRIs (high dose) + CBT with Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP). CBT alone can be effective. SSRIs alone: 40-60% response.

text: Most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage?
rationale: Uterine atony = #1 PPH cause (80%). 4 Ts: Tone (atony), Trauma, Tissue (retained placenta), Thrombin (coagulopathy). Tx: oxytocin first-line.

text: Drug of choice to prevent seizures in severe preeclampsia?
rationale: MgSO4 is drug of choice for seizure prevention (prophylaxis) AND treatment (eclampsia). Monitor for toxicity: loss of DTRs → respiratory depression.

text: Treatment for stable unruptured ectopic pregnancy?
rationale: Methotrexate (IM) for stable, unruptured ectopic with hCG <5000 and no cardiac activity. Surgery for rupture, instability, or failed MTX.

text: Gold standard screening test for colon cancer?
rationale: Colonoscopy = gold standard (detects and removes polyps). Screen starting age 50 (average risk) or 40 (first-degree relative with CRC).

text: Screening test for osteoporosis?
rationale: DEXA scan measures bone mineral density. Screen women ≥65 or postmenopausal <65 with risk factors. T-score: normal >-1, osteopenia -1 to -2.5, osteoporosis <-2.5.

text: Vaccine that prevents cervical cancer?
rationale: HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9) protects against strains 6,11,16,18 + 5 others. Given age 9-26 (ideally before sexual debut). Prevents 90% of cervical cancers.

text: A 58-year-old man presents with ST elevation in leads V1-V4. Most likely culprit artery?
rationale: Anterior STEMI (V1-V4) = LAD occlusion. Most dangerous MI. Can cause large anterior wall infarction, cardiogenic shock, and complete heart block.

text: A patient develops hypotension, distended neck veins and absent breath sounds on the left after trauma. Trachea deviates right. Diagnosis?
rationale: Tension pneumothorax: trachea deviates AWAY from affected side + absent breath sounds + hypotension. Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL) without waiting for CXR.

text: A 45-year-old patient on haloperidol for 7 years develops involuntary lip smacking and repetitive tongue movements. Diagnosis?
rationale: Tardive dyskinesia: chronic antipsychotic use → repetitive orofacial movements. Treatment: dose reduction, switch to clozapine, VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine).

text: A patient has fever, neck stiffness and photophobia. CSF shows neutrophils, low glucose, high protein. Most appropriate immediate treatment?
rationale: Bacterial meningitis: neutrophilic CSF + low glucose + high protein. START IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait for culture. Add dexamethasone.

text: A surgeon accidentally nicks a blood vessel during laparoscopic cholecystectomy. He repairs it successfully with no harm to the patient. What is his ethical obligation?
options: No disclosure needed if no harm occurred
Disclose the adverse event with an apology
Document only in chart without telling patient
rationale: Canadian ethics: physicians must disclose adverse events to patients even if no harm occurred. Apology is NOT a legal admission. Transparency builds trust and is legally required.

text: A 78-year-old with mild dementia refuses hip replacement surgery after a fall. He can explain the risks and alternatives. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Override refusal as he has dementia
Respect his refusal after capacity assessment
rationale: Capacity is decision-specific, not diagnosis-specific. If patient demonstrates understanding, appreciation, reasoning and communication = capable. Dementia does NOT automatically equal incapacity.

text: A physician discovers a bus driver patient has uncontrolled seizures and refuses to stop driving. What is the most appropriate next step?
rationale: Duty to protect overrides confidentiality when there is identifiable risk to the public. Physicians must report drivers with medical conditions that impair driving to licensing authorities (provincial regulations).
